title: Bayberry and soy blend candle
subtitle: A traditional winter container candle blending bayberry wax and soy wax with a bayberry fragrance oil
excerpt: Bayberry wax, rendered from the berries of the Myrica shrub, has a distinctive waxy-green herbal scent and a firmer texture than soy. At 30% in a soy blend, it gives the candle its character without the cost of pure bayberry. This is a traditional winter candle. A 7% fragrance load builds on the natural bayberry note.
sourceNotes: Soy wax container candle method: Bramble Berry candle-making guide (2023). Bayberry wax (Myrica pensylvanica): melting point 46 to 48°C; firmer than soy at room temperature. 30% bayberry blend with soy wax: practical balance of character and cost. Fragrance load 7% of combined wax weight. Pour temperature 58 to 62°C.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 candle (200 ml glass jar, ~150 g finished wax)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale wax and fragrance proportionally for multiple jars. Keep the 70:30 soy-to-bayberry ratio.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for at least 3 days before the first burn.
recipe.temperatureNote: Pour at 58 to 62°C. The bayberry wax raises the blend's melting point slightly above pure soy.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Two years away from direct sunlight. Fragrance fades after around two years; the wax blend is stable indefinitely.

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural wax extracted by boiling the berries of the Myrica shrub. Firmer and more expensive than soy wax, with a distinctive mild herbal-green scent. Traditionally used for Christmas candles in North America and the British Isles.
The percentage of fragrance oil by combined wax weight. At 7%, the fragrance oil builds on the bayberry wax's natural scent without overwhelming it.
The first burn. Allow the melt pool to reach the jar edge before extinguishing to set the candle's burn pattern.
When a candle burns straight down the centre, leaving unburned wax at the edges. Caused by extinguishing before the melt pool reaches the jar edge on the first burn.

content.text: is expensive to buy pure, but at 30% blended with container soy wax it gives a candle its character: a faintly herbal-green note and a firmer texture than all-soy. The remaining 70% soy provides reliable container adhesion and a clean burn. The 7%
of bayberry fragrance oil reinforces the natural wax note. This is a traditional winter candle.
Melt both waxes together over a simmering-water bath on low heat only. Never leave melting wax unattended. Keep a kitchen-fire blanket within reach.

items.prepNote: Container-grade soy wax, 70% of wax blend
30% of wax blend; melt together with the soy wax
7% of combined wax weight; add at 58 to 62°C
Pre-tabbed cotton wick for a 65 mm jar

content.text: Centre the pre-tabbed wick in the jar and hold in place with the wick-centring bar across the rim.

Weigh both waxes into the candle pitcher and melt together over a simmering-water bath, heating to 68°C. Remove from heat. When the wax cools to 58 to 62°C, add the fragrance oil and stir for 2 minutes. Pour slowly into the jar, leaving 10 mm headspace. Tap once to release air bubbles.
Leave undisturbed for 3 to 5 days. Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn.
. Allow the melt pool to reach the jar edge before extinguishing, about 3 to 4 hours. Extinguishing early causes
on subsequent burns. Trim the wick to 5 mm before every burn.
Two years away from direct sunlight. The bayberry-soy blend is stable. Store with a lid to protect the wax surface.
